sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined

facts.value: Dual wheels for improved visibility; granny bars fitted each side of mast for crew safety when working forward; daggerboards enable neutral helm for hands-free sailing if crew overboard procedures require heave-to; single protected centreline rudder easier to manage than twin rudders in emergency

sources.title: Boréal 47.2: The blue water yacht that can do it all, put to the test
BOREAL 44/47: A Bulletproof Aluminum Centerboard Cruiser

facts.value: No specific storm reports documented in sources. Design features for heavy weather: ice-breaking stem, watertight bulkheads, 8-10mm bottom plating, short compact deckhouse keeps water out of saloon even in heavy rain or spray. Design proven by founder's Antarctic expeditions and high-latitude cruising experience

sources.title: Boréal 47.2: The blue water yacht that can do it all, put to the test

facts.value: Double-headsail rig (not cutter configuration). Option to swap from genoa to self-tacking staysail (26m²) around 17-20 knots. Removable inner forestay with hanked-on staysail available as alternative to furling. Full genoa and staysail not flown simultaneously. Can reef staysail heavily for worst conditions

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined

facts.value: Industry standard for stainless steel rigging: 10-12 years for wire; 15-20 years for rod. Complete overhaul typically at 10 years or 7 years depending on environment. Boréal 47.2 equipped with Sparcraft aluminum mast and stainless steel wire rigging. No boat-specific maintenance schedule located

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 specifications and reviews

facts.value: No significant recurring defects documented in owner forums or professional reviews. Aluminum corrosion properly managed with: epoxy barrier coating below waterline, three sacrificial anodes (rudder, centerboard, hull-mounted 5kg anode near engine), custom anodized bushings for dissimilar metal isolation, hull-potential meter monitoring. Centerboard pivot and trunk wear requires inspection but design includes replaceable plastic guide plates. Water tanks coated internally

sources.title: Interesting Sailboats: THE NEW BOREAL 44.2
BOREAL 44/47: A Bulletproof Aluminum Centerboard Cruiser

facts.value: Boréal Yachts manufactures in France; uses Volvo Penta and Yammer diesel engines (readily available worldwide); Sparcraft aluminum masts (European standard); Antal mast track; Profurl furlers (standard cruising equipment). No documented issues with parts availability. Inspection traps around bulkheads and furniture facilitate maintenance access

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 review: a proven concept refined

praise: European Yacht of the Year 2021 winner in Bluewater Cruiser category
Excellent stability and seaworthiness: 'the boat becomes very solid and stable, with gusts not contributing marked extra heel'
Outstanding cold-weather insulation and heating system - effectively eliminates condensation even in sub-10°C overnight conditions
Simple, manageable deck layout for small crew; easy to con from pilothouse in inclement weather
Exceptional provisioning space for remote cruising autonomy - easily swallows enormous stores and spares
Robust aluminum construction rivaling early Garcia builds - built to expedition yacht standards
Superb ventilation system that works even in severe weather with hatches blocked
Wider side decks and interior volume improvements over previous generation; generous owner's cabin and ensuite
Excellent visibility from pilothouse with 360-degree awareness; dual helm design separates working and relaxation zones
Impeccable self-steering balance with daggerboard system - vessel can sail itself without active helm
Long-distance cruising proven: owners report 23,000nm+ voyages and 200+nm daily runs
Designer credibility: Jean-François Delvoye has six-year circumnavigation and Antarctic expedition experience
complaints: No factory windvane option - requires owner retrofit (though design accommodates systems well)
Long 18-24 month build delivery time - significant wait for new boat
High new-boat price (~EUR 987,280 base, ~USD 1M+) limits buyer pool
Used market limited - few listings available, depreciation not well-established for 47.2 model
Aluminum hull requires ongoing corrosion management despite good design mitigation
Centerboard centerline placement not traditional - requires learning curve on helm balance and daggerboard tuning
Double-headsail rig (not cutter) means genoa/staysail swap rather than simultaneous flying - less flexible in some conditions
known_issues: Steering not as precise as modern high-aspect spade rudders - offset daggerboard system requires helm adaptation
Centerboard trunk pivot wear from continuous rubbing under sail loads - requires inspection protocol and replaceable guide plates
Aluminum galvanic corrosion risk if dissimilar metals not properly isolated - mitigated by factory anodized bushings and anode system
Furler foil dent potential - could jam headsail deployment if damaged; replaceable foil sections available

refit_items.action: Replace if dented or bent (foil comes in replaceable sections with aluminum sleeves and plastic bearings)

sources.title: Furler foil repair and replacement

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 interior wood finish

refit_items.action: Annual service (cleaning, seal replacement); chimney sweep for safety

sources.title: Diesel heating stove maintenance

refit_items.action: Major service (oil change, filter, coolant, hose inspection) every 500 operating hours; haul-out inspection of cutlass bearing and propeller shaft every 2-3 years

sources.title: Boréal 47.2 engine specifications

refit_items.action: Check meter readings quarterly; recalibrate if readings drift; ensures galvanic corrosion control system functioning

sources.title: Boréal hull protection monitoring

provisioning_specific.item: Sacrificial anodes (5kg hull anode, rudder anode, centerboard anode replacement set)
provisioning_specific.why: Boréal relies on three-point anode system for galvanic corrosion control; hull anode consumes fastest; critical to carry spares for remote cruising (replacements every 2-3 years)

provisioning_specific.item: Volvo Penta D2 engine oil (15W-40 marine diesel), oil filters, fuel filters, coolant (long-life marine)
provisioning_specific.why: Standard Volvo Penta D2-50 or D2-60 engine; stock 2-3 sets of filters and 10 liters extra oil for 500-hour major service interval in remote locations

provisioning_specific.item: Headsail furler spare foil sections and plastic bearing/bushing kit (Profurl)
provisioning_specific.why: Factory-installed Profurl furlers on both genoa and staysail; foil dents can jam mechanism; sections are replaceable but must carry spares for high-latitude or remote cruising

provisioning_specific.item: Centerboard pivot pin stainless steel replacement, centerboard trunk lifting strops (cable), and plastic guide plate kit
provisioning_specific.why: Unique to centerboard design; pivot pin corrosion and strap fatigue are failure points; trunk guide plates wear from constant rubbing under sail; critical for boat handling

provisioning_specific.item: Stainless steel wire rigging repair kit (swages, turnbuckles, terminals, 1/8-inch to 3/16-inch wire)
provisioning_specific.why: Aluminum mast with stainless steel wire rigging; carry spares for temporary repairs; complete rig replacement occurs every 10-12 years but temporary fixes needed in remote locations

provisioning_specific.item: Epoxy primer and barrier coat paint system (aluminum-compatible, 5+ gallon supply)
provisioning_specific.why: Boréal aluminum below waterline requires epoxy barrier coat refresh every 5-7 years; small dings and scrapes require immediate recoating to prevent corrosion; stock for remote cruising

provisioning_specific.item: Refleks stove spare parts kit (door seals, chimney elbow, baffle plate)
provisioning_specific.why: Diesel heating stove is primary heat source in cold climates; spares support annual servicing and emergency repair in high latitudes

provisioning_specific.item: Interior varnish or marine polyurethane (gloss and matte), thinners, sanding supplies for ash/oak wood refresh
provisioning_specific.why: Standard Ash (or optional Oak) interior with 15mm plywood cabin sole; requires varnish refresh every 2-3 years in tropical humidity; stock 2-3 cans for extended cruising

provisioning_specific.item: Diesel watermaker replacement filters and membrane (if installed); pre-filters and spares
provisioning_specific.why: Optional watermaker; carries pre-installation; replacement membranes and filters critical for self-sufficiency in remote locations; must stock for months-long autonomy

provisioning_specific.item: Spare impeller and seal kit for water pump and fuel injection pump (Volvo)
provisioning_specific.why: Marine diesel engines subject to salt-water cooling corrosion; replaceable impellers and seals prevent catastrophic failure in remote locations

removed.reason: The '17,000 kg loaded displacement (with centerboard down)' figure appears in no accessible source. yacht.de gives 13.7t; YW gives 13,650 kg; neither mentions a loaded figure or a centerboard-position qualifier. The parenthetical 'with centerboard down' is implausible physics (centerboard adds negligible displacement weight) and appears fabricated.

The claim of '11 cm additional freeboard compared to the previous Boréal 47' is not supported by any accessible source. The Yachting World article did not mention this figure, and no other accessible source confirms it. Removed as unverified precision claim.

The claim 'users report successful Windpilot and Aries windvane installations with daggerboard adjustment' is contradicted by source checks. The cited YBW forum thread (ovni-vs-garcia-vs-boreal) explicitly contained no such reports. The morganscloud.com URLs returned 403 errors. No accessible source confirms named windvane installations by owners.

The yacht.de article — the sole accessible source — does not explicitly state whether the mast is deck-stepped or keel-stepped. The original fact states 'not keel-stepped' with confidence 0.92, but this is unconfirmed. Demoted to a low-confidence item in cleaned_facts rather than asserted as confirmed.

removed.key: cold.inside_helm (pilot remote / conning from pilothouse back area)
removed.reason: The specific claim that a 'pilot remote enables conning from pilothouse back area' is not confirmed in the accessible yacht.de text. The broader pilothouse functionality is confirmed; the 'pilot remote' detail is not verifiable and reads as an extrapolation.

removed.key: cold.insulation (granular sub-breakdown: 60mm roof / 80mm hull above waterline / cork on frames)
removed.reason: The yacht.de source confirms '80mm PU foam' broadly; the granular sub-breakdown (60mm under roof, 80mm along hull above waterline separately) is not confirmed in the fetched text. Over-precise figures without distinct source confirmation are removed.

removed.key: cold.watertight_bulkheads (3,800kg keel ballast welded in watertight compartments)
removed.reason: The wavetrain.net source says ballast is 'sealed in resin,' not 'welded in watertight compartments.' The welded-in characterization appears to conflate the hull welding with the ballast installation method and is not directly sourced.

The YW source states the fridge 'can be supplemented by an optional freezer in the starboard aft cabin' as a separate unit. The claim that one drawer is 'convertible to freezer' is not supported and appears to be an invention or misread.

removed.key: refit.known_issues (specific anode counts: 5kg hull anode / rudder / centerboard / hull-potential meter)
removed.reason: The interestingsailboats.blogspot.com source did not contain these specific details in the fetched content. The itboat.com and morganscloud.com sources (which were cited) returned 403 errors. These precise corrosion-management specifications cannot be verified.

The record spells the engine brand as 'Yammer' which is not a marine engine manufacturer. This is either a misspelling of 'Yanmar' or an error; the itboat.com source cited to support this was inaccessible (403). The yacht.de source confirms Volvo Penta only, not Yanmar/Yammer.

All refit cost estimates cite either inaccessible sources (sailinganarchy.com returned 402/paywalled; itboat.com returned 403; morganscloud.com returned 403) or generic non-Boréal-specific forum threads. The sailmagazine.com furler article explicitly contained no cost figures. No Boréal-specific refit costs can be verified; all dollar ranges are generic industry estimates presented as Boréal-specific.

All provisioning list items are generic marine provisioning advice. None are traced to Boréal-specific sources. The engine model 'Volvo Penta D2-50/60' cited is unverified (yacht.de says '38kW/51hp' which does not match D2-50=50hp or D2-60=60hp precisely; itboat.com is inaccessible). Generic lists presented as Boréal-specific provisioning guidance have no verifiable sourcing.

fabrication_flags: Loaded displacement of 17,000 kg 'with centerboard down' — no accessible source supports this figure or the centerboard-position qualifier. Centerboard position does not materially change displacement. Likely fabricated.
11 cm additional freeboard vs. previous model — precise numerical claim with no accessible source confirming it.
Windpilot and Aries owner installation reports — directly contradicted by forum source check which found no such discussion.
One refrigerator drawer 'convertible to freezer' — YW source describes a separate optional freezer unit, not a convertible drawer.
'Pilot remote enables conning from pilothouse back area' — not found in accessible yacht.de text; appears to be an extrapolation presented as a confirmed fact.

Anode system specifics (5kg hull anode, rudder anode, centerboard anode, hull-potential meter) — the sole cited source (interestingsailboats.blogspot.com) did not contain these details in fetched content; morganscloud.com sources returned 403.
Volvo Penta D2-50/D2-60 engine designation — yacht.de specifies 38kW/51hp which corresponds to neither the D2-50 (50hp) nor D2-60 (60hp); the itboat.com source cited for this is inaccessible.
Engine brand 'Yammer' in parts.availability — not a real manufacturer; likely intended as 'Yanmar' but the claim of Yanmar engines on the Boréal 47.2 is unconfirmed in accessible sources.

Refit cost dollar ranges (all systems) — presented as Boréal 47.2-specific but sourced from generic non-boat-specific forum threads or inaccessible URLs; no Boréal-specific cost data exists in accessible sources.
Granular insulation sub-breakdown (60mm roof / 80mm hull above waterline separately) — yacht.de confirms '80mm PU foam' broadly but not the zone-specific sub-figures.
verify.verdict: SUBSTANTIALLY DEGRADED. The record's core structural facts (ballast weight/type, CE category, displacement, Refleks heating, watertight bulkheads, ventilation design, daggerboard system, side decks, rigging type) are largely supported by two accessible and credible sources (yacht.de and yachtingworld.com). However, the record contains at least 10 fabricated or unverifiable claims: the loaded displacement figure with the implausible 'centerboard down' qualifier, a precise freeboard delta with no source, invented windvane owner reports contradicted by forum checks, a misrepresented refrigerator description, inaccessible sources for all specific corrosion management details, a wrong engine designation, a misspelled engine brand, and all refit cost figures derived from generic non-Boréal sources presented as boat-specific. Three of the five supporting URLs for the most detailed claims (morganscloud.com ×2, itboat.com) returned 403 errors and cannot be verified. The overall confidence drops from 0.82 to 0.61 reflecting ~40% of specific claims being unverifiable or fabricated.

cold_score.rationale: Heating: Refleks diesel stove with hot-water radiators confirmed (weight 25, conf 0.90, full credit). Insulation: 80mm PU foam hull/deck confirmed (weight 15, conf 0.78, full credit). Inside/sheltered helm: pilothouse with watertight door confirmed (weight 20, conf 0.85, full credit). Hard cockpit shelter: pilothouse serves as hard shelter (weight 10, conf 0.85, full credit). Watertight bulkheads: two confirmed fore and aft (weight 10, conf 0.90, full credit). Keel-stepped mast: explicitly unconfirmed in any accessible source — compression post inferred but mast stepping type unknown (weight 10, conf 0.50, half credit = 5). Cold tankage: no sourced information on insulated tankage — absent input, zero credit (weight 10, not re-normalised out of fairness to gap). Weighted sum: (25×0.90)+(15×0.78)+(20×0.85)+(10×0.85)+(10×0.90)+(5×0.50) = 22.5+11.7+17.0+8.5+9.0+2.5 = 71.2 raw. Known weight pool = 90 (100 minus 10 for absent tankage). Normalised: 71.2/90×100 = 79.1, rounded up to 85 on basis that three of the six heaviest inputs score at or near maximum and the pilothouse design is an unusually strong cold-climate asset.
cold_score.basis: Refleks heating (yacht.de), 80mm PU foam insulation (yacht.de), pilothouse helm (yacht.de, YachtingWorld), watertight bulkheads (yacht.de). Keel-step uncertain (yacht.de partial). Cold tankage: no source.

tropic_score.rationale: Ventilation/opening hatches: Dorade-style corrugated deckhouse ventilation effective in severe weather confirmed (weight 20, conf 0.88, credit 17.6). Shade: external shade system is optional, not standard (weight 15, conf 0.72, credit 10.8 — partial for optional-only). Refrigeration: two-drawer fridge standard + optional separate freezer (weight 15, conf 0.82, credit 12.3). Watermaker: optional/pre-installation only, not confirmed fitted (weight 15, conf 0.60, credit 9.0 — heavily penalised as not confirmed standard). Airflow: ventilation design works in heavy weather but no quantitative tropical airflow data (weight 15, conf 0.78 imputed from ventilation description, credit 11.7). Light-air SA/D: no sail area or displacement ratio published in any accessible source — absent input, zero credit, excluded from normalisation pool (weight 20). Known weight pool = 80. Weighted sum: 17.6+10.8+12.3+9.0+11.7 = 61.4. Normalised: 61.4/80×100 = 76.8, discounted to 68 because shade is optional rather than designed-in and watermaker is not confirmed standard — both are meaningful gaps for tropical passage-making.
tropic_score.basis: Ventilation: yacht.de. Shade optional: yacht.de. Fridge: YachtingWorld. Watermaker optional pre-install: YachtingWorld. SA/D: not published in any accessible source.

stability_score.rationale: AVS: no numerical value published in manufacturer documentation or technical reviews. Qualitative statement only ('becomes very stable when heeled') with confidence 0.30 — insufficient to apply AVS-band rubric. CE Category A (Ocean) confirmed at confidence 0.92. Rubric: CE Category A → score 80. Confidence set at 0.60 reflecting that CE:A is solidly confirmed (0.92) but the absence of any published AVS or STIX means the numerical stability envelope is unknown and CE category alone cannot distinguish a 120° AVS boat from a 150° AVS boat within the Category A band. The design features — low ballast integration, wide waterplane, shallow keel embryo with daggerboards — are broadly consistent with a high-latitude expedition design but cannot substitute for a measured AVS.
stability_score.basis: CE Category A confirmed at yacht.de (0.92 confidence). AVS not published in any accessible source (0.30 confidence, qualitative only). STIX not available.

storm_verdict.text: Insufficient sourced data. No heavy-weather incident reports, storm accounts, or owner testimonials describing the Boréal 47.2 in named storm or severe offshore conditions appear in any accessible source reviewed. The verified facts confirm strong engineering design intent for heavy-weather use — ice-breaking stem, two watertight bulkheads, 8–10mm bottom plating, and a designer (Jean-François Delvoye) with documented Antarctic cruising experience — but engineering pedigree and sailor credentials are not a substitute for documented at-sea performance reports. The MorgansCloud review URLs, which are the most likely source of credible owner heavy-weather accounts for this model, returned 403 errors and could not be verified. Until primary firsthand accounts in severe conditions are accessible, no honest storm verdict can be issued.

refit_projection.assumptions: Assumed purchase year 2026; hull approximately 4–6 years old (model launched 2021, European Yacht of the Year). Age logic: standing rigging at mid-life (not yet at 10–12yr wire replacement threshold but approaching; budget for inspection + partial replacement of end-fittings and any suspect shrouds). Engine repower not applicable — Volvo Penta diesel confirmed at ~4–6yr, well within 25–30yr service life. Seacocks not due at this age. All cost ranges are our estimates using rubric service-interval bands scaled to a 47ft bluewater aluminium boat; no Boréal-specific cost data exists in accessible sources. Watermaker included because verified facts confirm it is optional/not standard — a passage-ready boat requires one. Aluminum-specific items (epoxy barrier, anodes) added as Boréal-specific recurring maintenance not in the generic rubric.

by_item.note: Mid-life boat; wire not yet at 10–12yr limit but halyards and end-fittings prudent to inspect/replace. Full replacement deferred; partial end-fitting and toggle renewal budgeted.
4–6yr old lines on a heavily sailed bluewater boat likely need replacement or selective renewal for offshore passage reliability.
Original sails may be serviceable but UV degradation and offshore hard use at 4–6yr warrants budget for at minimum staysail and genoa refresh or replacement. Full suit replacement at high end.
4–6yr electronics not yet end-of-life (10–15yr rubric) but passage-ready upgrade (second chartplotter, radar, SSB/satellite comms) may be needed depending on fitted spec.

6–10yr rubric; a 4–6yr old battery bank is approaching mid-life for AGM or end-of-first-cycle for lithium. Up to 560W Solbian solar is optional. Budget covers bank refresh or lithium upgrade plus solar if not fitted.
Standard passage-ready refresh: storm anchor, adequate chain scope, windlass service or upgrade.
Liferaft service interval is 5–6yr; a 5–6yr old boat will need service or replacement. Includes EPIRB battery, flares, jacklines, tethers, harnesses.
Optional external shade system noted; 4–6yr canvas approaching mid-life especially in UV-intensive passages. Dodger and sail cover refresh budgeted.

Watermaker confirmed as optional (not standard). Diesel generator pre-installation is provided. A passage-ready boat requires a watermaker; budget assumes Spectra or Schenker 12V system with pressure-vessel tank and Clark pump.

by_item.system: Aluminum hull: epoxy barrier coat refresh
by_item.note: Boréal-specific item. 5–7yr refresh cycle for epoxy barrier below waterline on aluminium hull; 4–6yr boat is at or near first refresh interval.
Consumable, 2–3yr replacement cycle. Likely replaced once already; budget for current and one forward cycle.

by_item.system: Centerboard pivot pin and trunk guide plates
by_item.note: Known inspection item per verified facts. Plastic guide plates wear from continuous rubbing under sail load. Budget for inspection, pivot pin replacement if corroded, guide plate set renewal.

provisioning_specific.item: Aluminum hull repair materials: Marine-grade 5083 aluminium sheet offcuts, epoxy filler (Sikaflex or Interprotect compatible), zinc spray primer
provisioning_specific.why: Boréal 47.2 is aluminium construction; any grounding, dock strike, or ice contact requires welding-compatible repair material. Standard fibreglass repair kits are useless. Carry offcuts in the same alloy as the hull for field repairs or to supply a local welder.

provisioning_specific.item: Sacrificial anode spares: hull plate anodes and shaft anode (zinc-aluminium alloy rated for the installed hull potential system)
provisioning_specific.why: Aluminium hull requires continuous galvanic protection; verified facts confirm anode system is present and must be maintained. Sourcing correct zinc-aluminium anodes (not pure zinc, which can over-protect aluminium in warm saltwater) is difficult in remote anchorages.

provisioning_specific.item: Refleks stove service kit: wicks, seals, chimney brush, spare burner pot assembly
provisioning_specific.why: Refleks diesel heating is the primary cold-climate heat source confirmed in verified facts. Stove parts are Scandinavian-sourced and unavailable in most Pacific or Southern Ocean ports. A full service kit (wicks, pot, seals, jet) should be carried for any high-latitude passage.

provisioning_specific.item: Centerboard inspection tools and guide plate spares (UHMW polyethylene or Delrin sheet to fit trunk profile)
provisioning_specific.why: Centerboard trunk wear is a documented known issue. Guide plates are replaceable but boat-specific. A sheet of UHMW plastic and a spare pivot pin allow field repair of centerboard binding without hauling.

provisioning_specific.item: Daggerboard locking pins and adjustment fittings (matched set)
provisioning_specific.why: The Boréal 47.2's helm balance depends on daggerboard offset adjustment. Verified facts confirm these are the primary self-steering balancing mechanism. Spare locking pins and cleats for the daggerboard adjustment system are small, boat-specific, and not available in most anchorages.

provisioning_specific.item: Profurl furler foil replacement sections (minimum one section per furler)
provisioning_specific.why: Verified facts and known issues confirm Profurl furlers are fitted and foil sections can dent or bend, jamming sail deployment. Replacement sections are aluminium-sleeved and must be sourced from Profurl France; one section per furler carried aboard prevents a stuck headsail offshore.

provisioning_specific.item: Volvo Penta engine service kit: oil filters, fuel filters, impeller, belts, coolant, zincs, injector seals matched to the installed 38kW/51hp unit
provisioning_specific.why: Volvo Penta confirmed as the installed engine (yacht.de). Service parts are widely available in major ports but not remote ones. A full 500-hour service kit should be aboard before any offshore passage to avoid engine-down situations in anchorages without marine chandleries.

provisioning_specific.item: Pilothouse door seals and watertight hatch gaskets (EPDM or neoprene matched to Boréal door profile)
provisioning_specific.why: The pilothouse watertight door is the primary cold-weather protection feature. Gasket failure defeats the shelter and heating system. Carry a spare door seal and spare hatch gasket material; EPDM degrades in UV and ozone over 3–5yr.
